convinced four buddies to buy season tickets to the Cleveland Browns, which that would be a sales job. That would be a sales job. Yeah, to your second point, most business owners don't know where their business comes from, by the way. Right. Correct. I mean, it's pathetic. Yeah, they're just happy about it so they remember it. Well, yeah. Right. So the reason that they don't, so first is they actually think we're doing great. It's all we could get, right? Secondly, there's a profound sense of entitlement in professionals, business owners, sale people, etc. That so their, their thinking loop is I do a really good job at my core, you know, deliverable. So you come here, you get great dental care. You come here, you get a great meal. You come here, you get your haircut exactly the way it should be cut every single time. So you get your two pay washed, whatever it is. So I was gonna say something about your hair. Okay, I'm gonna let it go. I'm gonna let it go. I'll make something. So so I do a really good job with your core deliverable. On top of that, I provide really good service. Therefore, I am entitled to referrals. Therefore, I will get referrals because I get what I'm entitled to in a just universe. Right. Some people put in the third thing, which is I'm a really good guy, or they put in the fourth thing, which is I do really good job. I give really good service. I'm a really good guy. And I got five kids to feed. I have a need. Right. Therefore, I will get, you know, referrals. Whereas everything I just described is like sort of a minimum anti to get to the table. Right. It's not, you know, so there's that. There's this entitlement idea. Sometimes there's ego in the way that the act of asking for of doing anything overtly to stimulate referrals is a shadow on my ego. So I won't do it. There's that reason. Some people, it's their staff is running their business. And when they go to try and do anything proactive to stimulate referrals are really pretty much proactive to do anything else.
